title: Quantum Noise Models

theory.introduction: Real qubits never evolve in complete isolation. The environment leaks information, hardware drifts over time, and measurements are imperfect. A noise model gives us a structured way to describe how those physical imperfections distort an otherwise ideal quantum state.
theory.why_it_matters: Noise is the reason near-term quantum hardware cannot simply run deep circuits forever. If you understand what kind of error is happening, you can choose a better circuit, a better measurement basis, or an error-correction strategy that targets the real weakness instead of guessing.
theory.core_idea: A noise model is a mathematical rule for how an ideal state changes after an unwanted physical process. Different models damage different parts of the Bloch vector: some change classical populations, some erase phase information, and some do both.
key_points: Noise acts on amplitudes before you ever see the final measurement result.
Bit-flip and phase-flip errors are different even when one basis hides the difference.
Depolarizing noise shrinks the state toward a fully random mixture.
Amplitude damping models energy relaxation from |1> toward |0>.
Choosing the right measurement basis is often the difference between seeing the error and missing it.

bit_flip.description: A bit-flip channel applies an X-like error with probability p, swapping |0> and |1> information.
bit_flip.effect: Changes populations in the computational basis.
bit_flip.analogy: Like a classical wire that occasionally turns a 0 into a 1.
bit_flip.importance: Important for storage and readout because it directly changes the classical answer.

phase_flip.description: A phase-flip channel applies a Z-like error with probability p, changing relative phase but not computational-basis populations.
phase_flip.effect: Leaves Z-basis counts unchanged for many states while destroying interference.
phase_flip.analogy: The wave still has the same size, but one branch now points the wrong way.
phase_flip.importance: Critical in superposition-heavy algorithms because interference depends on phase being correct.

depolarizing.description: Depolarizing noise mixes the state with randomness, pushing the Bloch vector toward the center.
depolarizing.effect: Reduces confidence in every direction at once.
depolarizing.analogy: Instead of nudging the state one specific way, it blurs the whole picture.
depolarizing.importance: Useful as a compact model for many small imperfections acting together.

amplitude_damping.description: Amplitude damping models energy relaxation. The |1> component decays toward |0> over time.
amplitude_damping.effect: Pulls the Bloch vector upward toward |0> while also shrinking transverse coherence.
amplitude_damping.analogy: Like an excited atom emitting energy and falling back to its ground state.
amplitude_damping.importance: One of the most realistic channels for superconducting and trapped-ion style hardware.

mathematical_representation.bit_flip_channel: rho' = (1-p) rho + p X rho X
mathematical_representation.phase_flip_channel: rho' = (1-p) rho + p Z rho Z
mathematical_representation.amplitude_damping: Excited-state population relaxes toward |0> with probability p.

sections.title: Where Noise Comes From
content: Qubits couple weakly to their environment.
Longer circuits accumulate more opportunities for decoherence.

sections.title: Why Basis Choice Matters
content: A phase flip on |+> may look invisible in the Z basis because probabilities stay 50/50.
The same phase flip becomes obvious in the X basis because |+> turns into |->.
Noise is not just about the final bit count. It is also about what happens to coherence.
sections.intuition: If you only look in one basis, some errors can hide in plain sight.

sections.title: How To Fight Noise
content: Error mitigation estimates and subtracts some noise effects.
Quantum error correction spreads information across many qubits so a single fault can be detected.
sections.important_note: Noise cannot be removed by wishful thinking. It has to be modeled, measured, and engineered around.

examples.title: Bit flip on |0>
examples.description: Start in |0> and apply a bit-flip channel with error probability p.
steps: With probability p, the state flips to |1>.
examples.result: Z-basis probabilities become P(0) = 1-p and P(1) = p.
examples.insight: This looks very classical because the error changes the observed bit directly.

examples.title: Phase flip on |+>
examples.description: Start in |+> = (|0> + |1>) / sqrt(2) and apply a phase-flip channel.
steps: The Z basis still shows 50/50 because populations did not move.
The X basis changes because |+> can become |-> after a Z-like phase error.
examples.result: Interference is damaged even when the computational-basis histogram looks unchanged.
examples.insight: Phase noise is easy to underestimate if you only watch classical counts.

limitations: A simple noise model is still only an approximation of real hardware.
Different qubits on the same chip can have different error rates.
Correlated noise is harder to model than independent single-qubit errors.
summary: Noise models let us talk about hardware imperfections in a precise way. They explain whether an error changes classical outcomes, destroys phase information, or pushes the whole state toward randomness.

simulation.section_subtitle: Compare how different channels distort the same qubit state and see why some errors only appear once you inspect the right basis.
try_this: Compare phase flip on |+> in the Z basis and X basis.
Use amplitude damping on |1> and watch the Bloch point drift toward |0>.
Increase depolarizing strength and watch the state shrink toward the center.

quiz.question: Which noise model directly swaps |0> and |1> information?
quiz.explanation: A bit-flip channel acts like an unwanted X error, so it changes the computational-basis population directly.

quiz.question: Why can phase noise look invisible in the Z basis?
options: Because phase noise only affects the X and Y information, not always the Z-basis populations
Because the measurement device ignores all noise
Because phase noise is always corrected automatically
quiz.answer: Because phase noise only affects the X and Y information, not always the Z-basis populations
quiz.explanation: Relative phase can change while the probability of measuring 0 or 1 stays the same, so the damage appears only when you inspect coherence-sensitive bases.

quiz.question: What does depolarizing noise do to the Bloch vector?
options: Rotates it perfectly around the Z axis
quiz.explanation: Depolarizing noise mixes the state with randomness, so the Bloch vector loses length and drifts toward the origin.

quiz.question: Which channel best models energy relaxation from |1> back to |0>?
quiz.explanation: Amplitude damping captures the physical idea of an excited qubit losing energy and relaxing toward its ground state.
